front: War: ended in 1648 with the Peace of Westphalia, having begun in 1618
War: a religious conflict fought between Protestants and Catholics within the Holy Roman Empire
War: killed an estimated third to half of Germany's population
War: had distinct "Danish" and "Swedish" phases following the Bohemian Revolt
War: the earliest known depiction of Scots soldiers wearing tartan dates from this conflict
War: the first English-language periodicals, known as corantos, brought news from this conflict
Event: Catholic officials Jaroslav von Martinitz and Wilhelm von Salvata were thrown from a window of Hradčany Castle in Prague, sparking this war
work: Causes: Defenestration of Prague & Bohemian Revolt

front: Ruler: this zealously Catholic Habsburg, whose officials were defenestrated in Prague, was named heir by his childless cousin Emperor Matthias
work: Causes: Defenestration of Prague & Bohemian Revolt

front: Law: this edict attempted to restore Catholic ecclesiastical property under the rule of "ecclesiastical reservation" before later being revoked
work: Causes: Defenestration of Prague & Bohemian Revolt

front: Battle: Count Tilly's Catholic League army defeated the Bohemian army of the "Winter King" Frederick V here in 1620
Battle: 27 prisoners were executed in Prague's Old Town Square after this 1620 Catholic League victory
Battle: was the war's first major battle
Event: Catholic forces under Count Tilly and Albrecht von Wallenstein burned this Protestant city in 1631, killing roughly 20,000 inhabitants
Event: Croatian and Walloon mercenaries, including former Habsburg uskoks, earned particular notoriety during this 1631 atrocity
Event: mercenary Peter Hagendorf's diary records his participation in this 1631 atrocity
Battle: Gustavus Adolphus's Swedish army used an innovative oblique battle order on its left flank to rout opposing tercios here
Battle: Gustavus Adolphus won this battle immediately after the sack of Magdeburg, turning Sweden's intervention into a major victory
Battle: Finnish Hakkapeliitta cavalry fought for Sweden here in 1632, mortally wounding Johann Tserclaes, Count of Tilly
Battle: Gustavus Adolphus, assisted by chancellor Axel Oxenstierna, was killed here by the forces of Albrecht von Wallenstein
Event: the Protestant Heilbronn League formed after Gustavus Adolphus's death in this 1632 battle
Battle: Spanish tercios won a rare victory here in 1634, in contrast to their earlier defeat at Breitenfeld
Battle: the Catholic forces of Cardinal-Infante Ferdinand and Ferdinand of Hungary defeated the Protestant army of Gustav Horn here in 1634
War: the neutrality of the Old Swiss Confederacy was threatened by the Bündner Wirren, a theater of this conflict that also included battles at Breitenfeld and Nördlingen
Battle: France's Duc d'Enghien (later the Prince of Condé) defeated Spain here in 1643, just days after Louis XIV took the French throne
Battle: this 1643 French victory crushed Spanish military dominance in the war's later phase
Battle: this clash marked the first great defeat of the Spanish tercio, a square infantry formation of up to 3,000 men
War: Jörg Jenatsch's revolt over the murder of Pompeius Planta sparked this Alpine conflict over control of the Valtellina passes, suppressed by Henri de Rohan
work: Offshoot Theaters: Bündner Wirren & War of the Mantuan Succession

front: Figure: this French Huguenot commander suppressed the Bündner Wirren, having earlier forced the Treaty of Montpellier through his own revolts
work: Offshoot Theaters: Bündner Wirren & War of the Mantuan Succession

front: Figure: this Swiss rebel sentenced the head of the Planta family to be pinned to a floor with an axe, then was later axe-murdered in revenge by a man dressed as a bear
work: Offshoot Theaters: Bündner Wirren & War of the Mantuan Succession

front: War: caused by the deaths of two heirless Gonzaga dukes, this conflict triggered southern Europe's last major bubonic plague outbreak
work: Offshoot Theaters: Bündner Wirren & War of the Mantuan Succession

front: Figure: financed his armies by forcing civilians to pay "contributions," popularizing the phrase "bellum se ipsum alet" ("war will feed itself")
Figure: profited from the Kipper- und Wipperzeit, a scheme debasing currency and pumping it into neighboring economies
Figure: was betrayed by subordinate Ottavio Piccolomini and assassinated at Cheb by Walter Devereux, with Scottish officer Walter Leslie among the plotters
Figure: the best account of his assassination is a "holographic account" by a Scottish mercenary, whose countrymen pioneered the close-range "Highland Charge" tactic used in the plot
Ruler: brought Sweden into the war, assisted by his chancellor Axel Oxenstierna
Ruler: his soldiers often sang his favorite battle hymn, "Fear Not, O Little Flock, the Foe"
Treaty: signed simultaneously at Osnabrück and Münster in 1648, this settlement is credited with establishing the doctrine of state sovereignty in international law
Play: Anna Fierling's daughter Kattrin is shot while beating a drum to warn a town
back: Mother Courage and Her Children (Bertolt Brecht)

front: Play: Anna Fierling's son Swiss Cheese is killed for hiding a paybox
back: Mother Courage and Her Children (Bertolt Brecht)

front: Play: Helene Weigel premiered this play's title role
back: Mother Courage and Her Children (Bertolt Brecht)

front: Play: the title character sings "The Song of the Great Capitulation"
back: Mother Courage and Her Children (Bertolt Brecht)

front: Play: the title character declines to file a complaint against a Captain
back: Mother Courage and Her Children (Bertolt Brecht)

front: Play: Anna Fierling's son Eilif is also killed
back: Mother Courage and Her Children (Bertolt Brecht)

front: Play: the prostitute Yvette sings "The Fraternization Song"
back: Mother Courage and Her Children (Bertolt Brecht)

front: Play: a girl dreams of marriage while wearing a prostitute's hat and boots
back: Mother Courage and Her Children (Bertolt Brecht)

front: Play: a stabbed captain leaves a peasant his lucky (and loaded) dice
back: Mother Courage and Her Children (Bertolt Brecht)

front: Play: two characters trick a group of commanders into drunkenly swearing an oath of allegiance to a general instead of to a king
back: Mother Courage and Her Children (Bertolt Brecht)

front: Play: dramatizes Wallenstein's betrayal by Ottavio Piccolomini, including the doomed romance between his daughter Thekla and Max Piccolomini
Novel: the title yokel dreams of a giant oak tree with Mars sitting atop it
Novel: the protagonist lives with mermen under a lake
Novel: the protagonist becomes known as the "Hunter of Soest"
Novel: the protagonist meets Jupiter
Novel: the title character is conscripted to fight on both sides of the war
Novel: the protagonist eventually becomes a hermit
Novel: opens with a tightrope walker's trick that causes townspeople to fight over their shoes
Novel: features a talking donkey named Origenes
Author: wrote the hymn "Now Thank We All Our God" while conducting as many as 50 funerals a day during a wartime plague
Event: poets Andreas Gryphius and Paul Gerhardt wrote during this war
Event: an orphaned poet lamented "So, now we are destroyed; utterly; more than utterly!" by the "maddening music of war" in the poem "Tears of the Fatherland"
Author: wrote a history of this war at age 28
Event: Johann Georg Fuchs von Dornheim built a special "witch-prison" to torture and house accused witches during this wartime persecution
Event: Sweden looted this giant illuminated manuscript, the largest in the world, from Prague as war booty
Event: this 6th-century Gothic-language Bible was taken from Prague by Sweden as war booty
Figure: high taxes levied to fund the war effort provoked this Neapolitan fisherman's 1647 revolt against Habsburg rule
Concept: historian Arno Mayer treated the two World Wars as a single "second edition" of this conflict, terming the comparison the "Thirty Years' Crisis"
Battle: Christian IV of Denmark's allies suffered an early defeat here
Battle: Christian IV of Denmark was routed here after his allies' earlier defeat at Dessau, forcing his withdrawal from the war
Battle: Ernst von Mansfeld and Christian of Brunswick lost this early engagement
Figure: this Scottish commander left the war to contest Scotland's Bishops' Wars, after repelling a siege at Stralsund
